channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.mmorpg.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.mmorpg.com

item.title: Here's What's Coming In Path of Exile 2's Huge 0.5.0 Patch, With A New Campaign, Endgame Revamps, In-Game Build Guide And More
item.content:encoded:
Today, the Path of Exile 2 team unveiled what’s to come in the upcoming 0.5.0 patch. Titled “Return of the Ancients,” this content patch brings the usual campaign and new items—but massive endgame changes, new ascendencies, and even an in-game build guide are on the way as well. 

It’s worth noting that this is the last major content patch before Path of Exile 2’s team goes into grinding out the full 1.0 version, coming sometime after ExileCon happens in November. There’ll be balance patches and fixes as well along the way, but unless this patch’s content goes terribly wrong somehow, this will be it for now

There’s a lot to see in this new patch, so let’s get into it. 

Runes of Aldur Campaign 

The reveal stream has a lot of spoilers—but the long and short of what you need to know is that you follow the growth of a man named Farrow, who is trying to replicate the ancient art of Ezomyte Runesmithing. Of course, you’re dragged along for the ride as you find runes, explore new locales, and meet new allies and foes.

As you travel along the campaign, you get a chance to inscribe symbols and runes to get items, but the power awakens the undead nearby, forcing you to fight them, sometimes with new quirks based on what you’ve inscribed. As you go along, Farrow learns how to inscribe runic power into your items; you’ll also be buffed with the Runic Ward, which lets you endure past one death, though more powerful versions will have a cost. Other powers can be imbued into items and even skills as well later on. 

Later on, if you opt to do the Expedition League in the endgame, you’re given further content to literally explore. In the south of the endgame Atlas, you’ll be able to explore new regions throughout the sea using expanded Logbook capabilities; after completing an initial questline, you’ll be able to endlessly explore these ocean maps, many of which come with new obstacles and bosses. 

As they sum up, you’ll be taking on over 30 new monsters, plenty of new quests, over 30 new rune modifiers and 100 new runes to customize stuff, tons of new endgame maps, and the usual slurry of new currency. 

Massive Endgame Changes—Revamped Atlas, Updated Leagues, And New Stories

Once you’re past the campaign, it’s been quite easy to get lost in the massive, sprawling maps of the endgame, as the team acknowledges. Now, however, a huge endgame revamp aims to make it easier to follow along. 

As soon as you get out of the campaign, a new Fortress storyline guides you into an overhauled Atlas, which lets you explore the map in any which direction. The Atlas tree sees some overhauls, including its league nodes, that allow the team to integrate more flexible perks and quirks. 

For more bonuses yet, players can now take on apprenticeship under one of the new Atlas Masters. Leveling up one of these three new systems allows players to take on additional perks, though usually at a pretty hefty price. And there’s no need to commit to one, either, as players can easily swap between them as they go along. 

GGG also opts not to leave other leagues in the dust, as the Delirium, Breach and Ritual leagues get some more-than-minor revamps as well. Each has been given a new questline, which streamlines players through these leagues—which all also see some mechanic changes to each as well. 

There's a lot to take in, so we’ve rounded up all of the big endgame changes coming for you separately to peruse. 

New Asendancies, Items And More 

To further expand the action, two new Ascendancies are introduced in Return of the Ancients: The Spirit Walker for the Huntress, and the Martial Artist for the Monk. 

The Spirit Walker is exactly what it sounds like—players are capable of tapping into ancient Azmerian spirits that channel the spirits of the Stag, Owl, and Bear (or even local bosses?). These different “wisps,” accessed through the unlockable nodes, tap into different types of playstyles—Primal, Vivid, or Wild. The stag unlocks waves of “Stampedes” when attacking; the Owl tacks on new projectile feathers and enhances projectile speed; and the Bear is just a bear, which mauls, slams, and debuffs your foes. 

A new Hidden power also lets you combine the power of the three, and a special power lets you get perks for using Idols. And further down the node tree, players can literally Tame Beast Bosses, which lets you literally borrow a local boss for combat. 

The Martial Artist Ascendancy for Monk, meanwhile, lets players tap into three of the seven Hollow techniques, letting players control the flow of the world around them better. The Hollow Focus technique manifests Spiritual Energy into objects, such as Bells that can damage enemies around you and stone that mitigate damage. 

Down the line, a Martial Artist can literally embed runes into their bodies instead of into items for more power yet. The Way of the Stonefist concludes in a perk that changes gloves into a new base type and re-modifies the original items into having more powerful perks.

And as always, there are a ton of new items to tap into—and many have special perks that players will want to get their hands on. The Raven’s Flock is aimed at minion players, letting players use the Command Skill Mad Flight to create a flock of deadly ravens. Horror’s Flight are gloves that imbue you with stacks of Fear Incarnate, plus add the skill Crushing Fear, like the Monk’s Killing Palm except with Chaos Damage and scaling damage. 

Players will also want to get their hands on the Voices Jewel—which is a Jewel that grants you even more Jewel spots, with the number going up to even four slots, granting three extra. 

In-Game Build Guides And More Quality Of Life 

Alt-tab-peeking for your guides may soon be no more, as this Path of Exile 2 update introduces embedded in-game custom build guides. Through a new custom file format, players can import build guides, and in-game indicators mark which nodes, Ascendancies, Skill Gems and more you should be taking based on your desired end result. The GGG team has already reached out to some community leaders to get these set up, so we may see a few from day one, including Maxroll.gg. 

If you’ve ever been stuck running circles around the map looking for objectives (as I have), they’ve taken note and are looking to make it easier. The campaign now includes thematically-relevant visual indicators to follow so that you can go straight to that map’s objective if you so wish—some of which is embedded in the ground, and some of which are dynamic, like flying locusts. 

While we get more into endgame elsewhere, it’s worth noting that they also try to make it a lot more approachable, with new UI tweaks that show you your progress in accomplishing endgame league objectives. Challenges also make a grand return, and, very importantly, you can now shift-click to see items’ prices in the marketplace and toggle mod pricing. 

While normally, the reveal streams happen about a week before the release, these announcements come earlier than usual. All of these additions and changes come to Path of Exile 2 on May 29, with patch notes arriving on May 21 instead.

Victoria Rose

Victoria has professionally written about games since 2016, including small former tenures with Polygon and Fanbyte. She mostly spends time in FFXIV, head-deep in roleplay campaigns or stubbornly playing Black Mage through high-end raids. Former obsessions include Dota 2 and The Secret World (also mostly roleplaying). Come visit their estate: Diabolos (Crystal DC), Goblet, Ward 4, Plot 28.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/heres-whats-coming-in-path-of-exile-2s-huge-050-patch-with-a-new-campaign-endgame-revamps-in-game-build-guide-and-more-2000138012
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Massive Endgame Overhauls Are Coming To Path of Exile 2's Next Patch, With Revamped Atlas, Mechanics And Storylines
item.content:encoded:
New map, trees and stories arrive

The endgame of any action RPG such as the iconic Path of Exile series can be make or break—and Grinding Gear Games has decided to tackle it in one big swoop. 

As a huge part of the next and final content update in the ARPG’s Early Access stage, Path of Exile 2’s upcoming 0.5.0 patch, titled “Return of the Ancients,” features a huge, sweeping overhaul to the endgame. Not only are certain systems being changed up and refined, including the entire Atlas map itself, but many are receiving new questlines to make following the journey to power easier. 

As unveiled during today’s reveal stream, here’s a summary of what’s being changed up. 

PoE2’s Atlas Gets An Overhaul—Introducing The Fortress and Atlas Masters 

Most importantly, and most visibly off the bat, the endgame Atlas maps are being revamped into a new fully-explorable super-map starting on and centered around the Fortress, which has emerged after the cataclysms of the Beast in Wraeclast. Now, after emerging through the deadly traps and new mechanics of the Fortress itself, players can explore these maps in any direction, letting players take objectives of different endgame leagues at any pace they wish.  

In this new system, the Atlas Tree sees huge changes as well, tasking you with unlocking nodes by beating down bosses along the way. You’ll earn points each time you complete a map in the fortress, letting you progress simply by playing the endgame as usual 

New mechanics arrive with this rework—of course there are strongbox modifiers, but you can allow more and more difficult Shrines to appear instead of a boss, or giving you a shrine buff before entering a boss arena. Mechanics can also be combined, like letting an Azmeri spirit possess strongboxes to send more rares in the waves that follow, or by letting two faction leaders exist in an area. 

On top of all of these changes that Fortress brings, PoE2 is also introducing the Atlas Masters, which they effectively describe as an “Ascendancy class for your Atlas tree.” You get to choose between three new Masters that’ll help guide your playstyles, at a cost; for example, Jado of the Order of the Djinn will give extra modifiers on waystones but send you to a random location, and Hilda of the Monster Hunters will send Azmerian spirits from Rare monsters into Unique Bosses instead. 

Given the uniqueness of the perks (and drawbacks) of each system, once you unlock them, you can easily toggle between them before you enter the map through a special side menu on the endgame screen. 

On top of all of these, there are also new Expedition maps that players will be able to explore through the standard 0.5.0 Runes of Aldur campaign. Once you get the Logbook, you can progress the quest until it outright opens up a slew of islands in the south of the map with more mechanics and items to tackle. 

Overhauls And New Stories For Older Endgame Content—Delirium, Breach and Ritual

As part of the mission to tighten things up, some older endgame content has not only been revisited for format, but also for story in order to create a more comprehensive path to follow. 

Delirium gets a new questline called the “The Hare and the Raven,” where a mage freed from Tangmazu’s nightmares pins you as responsible for freeing The Raven Trickster (and he’s technically not wrong) and says you have to chase him down. 

As you go to find the Trickster, more mechanics are unlocked, including Liquid Emotions to mod your gear with, colored shards with different effects on enemies, and Delirium Fog that can sweep the map. Plus, a new gauge shows you your progress in navigating a Delirium map, and there’s a new Pinnacle Boss to tackle as well. 

Not only back by popular demand from the first Path of Exile title, but also integrated into PoE2’s endgame, the Breach path sees a similarly large upgrade with new mechanics as the Keepers of the Flame struggle to fight back against the Hiveborn. 

Now, players can collect “Hiveblood” to feed the iconic Genesis Tree with and “Wombgifts” that’ll grow new items and nodes to use in the war. Players can use 15 slots at a time to grow out their build in this instance, but these are refreshable with zero cost. There are also some new mechanics within the findable breaches themselves, including its own timer, a new phase, and more Breach Hives spawning around the new Fortress maps. 

Last but certainly not least, the Ritual gets the “Rite of the Nameless” questline that tasks players with helping a lost spirit named Aoife free herself from the nameless King of Mists—and quelling the source of his power, an ancient being of darkness, Bodach. To confront Bodach, you need to become “nameless” yourself by finding effigies throughout the maps and completing rituals. This can be done repeatedly, with the difficulty of the effigy search and the rituals growing with each playthrough. 

General PoE2 Endgame Changes 

Overall, Grinding Gear Games is looking to make the endgame in Path of Exile 2 more approachable, as once you get out of that campaign, it’s been something of a daunting task to get started. As mentioned before, the new format of the Atlas also players explore the endgame maps at any pace and in any order they wish in order to. 

To guide players through these various endgame stories, players are immediately given quests to tackle and pursue. More importantly, there’s a new endgame UI that lets players keep track of their progress through these endgame leagues and questlines so you can hop between them as you see fit (and as you gear up further). 

Challenges also make their big return in Path of Exile 2, with the reward this time being the Knight of Aldur armour set. Players can also show off their challenge completion via an in-chat display, as well as a hideout statue. 

All of these changes and updates are literally just the endgame updates. The “Return of the Ancients” update for Path of Exile 2 brings a new league as well, as always, plus new Ascendancies, new Uniques, and even an in-game build guide.

Given the scale of this update, this update arrives to Path of Exile 2 on May 29, with the patch notes coming out one week before, on May 21. 

Victoria Rose

Victoria has professionally written about games since 2016, including small former tenures with Polygon and Fanbyte. She mostly spends time in FFXIV, head-deep in roleplay campaigns or stubbornly playing Black Mage through high-end raids. Former obsessions include Dota 2 and The Secret World (also mostly roleplaying). Come visit their estate: Diabolos (Crystal DC), Goblet, Ward 4, Plot 28.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/massive-endgame-overhauls-are-coming-to-path-of-exile-2s-next-patch-with-revamped-atlas-mechanics-and-storylines-2000138010
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Path of Exile 2 Will Get Full 1.0 Release After ExileCon, After Its Next And Final Content Patch
item.content:encoded:
Over a year after its December 2024 launch into Early Access, Path of Exile 2’s team is apparently preparing it, for real, for a 1.0 full launch—though not for a few more months, until “some time after ExileCon.” 

In discussing the upcoming 0.5.0 launch in the recent patch announcement livestream, Game Director Jonathan Rogers explains that the patch will be the final major content patch for the Early Access version of the game, especially as it intends on righting the path of its endgame. However, it won’t come up anytime soon, as the developers need to do a little more legwork to get things going. As a result, there also won’t be any more major content patches until then.  

“Now normally we would do our next big content release in August, but in the run up to full release, our plan is to take a little longer on this one,” Rogers explains. “As big as 0.5.0 is, 1.0 is even bigger, so we will be launching 1.0 this year some time after Exilecon.” Of course, there’ll be some balancing patches and bug fixes as feedback pours in throughout its lifespan. 

As he explained in a press preview Q&A, though, the 1.0 release is also pending that the game is in a launchable state—not just to the developers, but the players as well, pending feedback from 0.5.0 and subsequent fixes. 

He also stated in the Q&A that not all weapons and classes will be out at the time of release, though there’ll certainly be more than there already are (we already have the hotly-anticipated Druid class as of December). Plus, the rest of PoE2’s base campaign will be included with the release, which Rogers says in the announcement is accompanied by “some of the coolest content that we have been sitting on for a while now.” 

What we also know is that the departure from Early Access will likely mean that Path of Exile 2 will join its predecessor in being a free-to-play title, as the $29.99 access key pricing was intended to be exactly that—a way to get in early and help support development financially and through actual gameplay. 

As a point of reference for that possible release, ExileCon is taking place the weekend of November 7 out in the studio’s homeland of New Zealand. Maybe we’ll get a holiday miracle. 



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/path-of-exile-2-will-get-full-10-release-after-exilecon-after-its-next-and-final-content-patch-2000138009
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Steam Controller Reservations Open Tomorrow, Giving You Another Chance To Get Your Hands On Valve's Controller
item.content:encoded:
Valve is opening a reservation queue to give people a chance to buy their highly anticipated Steam Controller, which sold out in less than an hour earlier this week.

The Steam Controller is Valve's update to its first controller attempt from over ten years ago (albeit with the same name), and I think many of us expected it to sell out right away when it went live earlier this week. I don't think any one expected it to sell out that quickly - though, scalpers gonna scalp I guess.

So Valve is opening a Reservation Queue to try to make it easier for everyone who actually wants one to get their hands on the controller at MSRP. The reservation queue opens tomorrow morning at 10am PT/1pm ET, and those who land in the queue will have their spot, well, reserved, so they can buy the controller when their time comes. 

Via Valve's post on Steam today:

"Here are the details on how we're hoping to improve the experience overall while also taking steps to limit reseller activity:

Reservation details:

  • Reservations will be limited to one Steam Controller per user.

  • Once you receive an order email, you have three days (72 hours) to make your purchase on Steam.

  • For now, customers who have already purchased a Steam Controller are not yet eligible to reserve another Steam Controller."

I do like that Valve is putting in a limit here to ensure both scalpers can't buy multiple at once, and that it's keeping accounts who have already purchased one away from this queue altogether. There is also an age requirement for your Steam account before you can buy the controller, meaning scalpers can't simply spin up multiple accounts this week to circumvent this rule. 

No hard date was given on when these reserved units will begin to ship, but Valve does state that it will start "fulfilling reservations next week" in the US and Canada regions, with UK, EU and AU to follow.



https://www.mmorpg.com/hardware/steam-controller-reservations-open-tomorrow-giving-you-another-chance-to-get-your-hands-on-valves-controller-2000138011
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Dune: Awakening Teases New Polar Cap Endgame Map With New Mechanics
item.content:encoded:
A briefly-mentioned piece of new content gets a little more detail, as Funcom has given some more foundational information on the “Polar Cap” map coming eventually to Dune: Awakening. 

Funcom first addresses the fact that the Polar Cap was mentioned at all as free content for the Chapter 3 DLC, but the team wanted to shift priorities towards fixing the game’s overall systems. Indeed, the team has implemented a lot over the past year, from server transfers to the recent new PvE Deep Desert maps, and self-hosted servers are getting a test go with the new update. 

The proper description of the Polar Cap map seems to justify the detour a little bit, as it’s a full-on new endgame map that “introduces new survival mechanics, new locations, new types of resources, new loot, new stuff to craft, and a new standalone storyline.” Given it’s an arctic-themed area, the requirements for survival will look different, providing a new challenge for those looking for fresh gameplay.

An embedded video of in-development footage in the full blog post also provides some literal insight, showing off icy terrains, blizzards, dungeons, and outposts (including some set on fire, which must be quite warm). 

That’s all the information we get for now, except that more information is coming soon, though we now know that it's an entirely new map with some new systems. For now, we’re awaiting the 1.4.0 patch, which just hit the public test servers.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/dune-awakening-teases-new-polar-cap-endgame-map-with-new-mechanics-2000138005
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Temtem: Pioneers Reveals Cute Bat Tem Dracub and Its Evolution, Drackle
item.content:encoded:
Temtem: Pioneers,  with under two days left in the Kickstarter campaign, has another new Temtem reveal. After teasing this one before, now we meet the bat-inspired Tem, Dracub, and its evolution, Drackle.

It’s a bat known for its laugh, personality, and fierce winds. Dracub's name is taken from Dracula + cub. Yes, Crema is on the nose here, but it is a cute batty nose. Dracub is an Earth/Wind type, and like Dracula, prefers darker environments.  So it's no surprise you'll find this one in caves all around the Downbelow. Train it, improve it, and give it some pets. 

Eventually, with enough care, you’ll get a Drackle. A fiercer bat, also with a Dracula related name (+ cackle, because again, they like to laugh) and an Earth/Wind type. Drackle is bigger, fast, and can strike enemies with powerful winds. That wingspan says it all. And with a Drackle, you can laugh with it as your enemies get windswept.

Even better? It’s one of the game’s gliding mounts. 

With this, the team has been showing off the Tems we've seen in the announcement trailer. Only some of the new additions that we can expect when the game releases, and Crema says they will introduce a few more over the next few months.  



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/temtem-pioneers-reveals-cute-bat-tem-dracub-and-its-evolution-drackle-2000138007
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: LVLUP Expo 2026: Highlights From The MIX - Side Quests
item.content:encoded:
In late April, the LVLUP Expo was held in Las Vegas.  This yearly event is a celebration of all things nerdy. From video games to anime, comic books, and trading card games, there was something for every brand of nerd.  While the actual numbers aren’t available yet, attendance was expected to surpass the 144,000 people who attended in 2025.  In one corner of the expo sat the Media Indie Exchange, aka the MIX. The MIX had titles from indie game developers. Some of these titles were new, but most of them were still unreleased.  I had the chance to play some of these titles, though, with so many people constantly waiting to play, I didn’t get the chance to play every single title.  Here are four of the titles that I thought were worth a second look.

Editor's Note: In this round-up, we have the gameplay impressions from two writers who attended the show for us: Michael Bradford-Christian and Justin Harmon. Instead of posting two smaller articles, we combined them into one. The first few games will be from Mike BC, while Justin's impressions will come after. - Bradford

Table Flip Simulator

I’ve got to start strong.  Table Flip Simulator from YummyYummyTummy and PM Studios starts with the premise that it’s your birthday and your boss doesn’t care.  He gives you an 83-second break, and that’s enough to send you into a rage…perhaps a table-flipping rage.

Table Flip Simulator was easily the funniest game I played the whole weekend.  My son and I stood there laughing our heads off at the completely absurd gameplay.  I watched him take out a whole window with a roll of toilet paper.  On another level, he was working at a grocery store and took out an elderly customer with a watermelon.  

In each level, you earn money through completing objectives and destruction.  I can’t stress enough how important it is to destroy everything.  Yes, some objectives are more peaceful, and there’s a way to hand an old woman her watermelon instead of throwing it at her, but there is no downside to just destroying everything instead.  Money can then be used to unlock styles and avatars, further customizing your gameplay.  While it felt a bit mobile-game-adjacent, I was extremely happy overall.  Table Flip Simulator will be released on PC and all current-gen consoles on May 21st.

Croak

Croak is a precision platformer from WoodRunner Games. It features art and animation from artists who have worked on Cuphead and Rick & Morty.  Croak is about a cursed prince trying to make his way back to the castle.  More accurately, the kingdom is cursed, and as part of that curse, the prince is now a frog.  

The gameplay mechanics are very simple. In just the first few minutes of the game, you can learn everything about how to move your regal amphibian. Despite an easy learning curve, Croak is also incredibly difficult. If you don’t land a move just right, you get backed up to the last checkpoint. Fortunately, if the demo is any indication, the checkpoint is very forgiving.

That’s important because Croak is the kind of game that makes me want to pull my hair out sometimes, with how insanely difficult it is.  This is not unique to the genre, but Croak gives you a false sense of security that all will be well by luring you in with cartoon graphics and an adorable frog.  But after hitting spikes the same color as the pink stuff under the fridge one too many times, you realize it’s called a precision platformer for a reason. As of right now, Croak only has a release window of 2026, but a demo is currently available on Steam.

Toxic Crusaders

If you remember the 90s, you probably remember games in the arcade or on the NES like Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles or Battletoads. Perhaps you were more of a SEGA player and had games like Golden Axe or Streets of Rage. Speaking of SEGA, Toxic Crusaders is a beat-em-up game by Retroware that acts as a spiritual successor to the 1992 SEGA Genesis title by the same name. These were games in which you progressed through a level and beat up guys along the way before getting to an end-of-level boss fight. 

While I don’t have much experience with the original Toxic Crusaders game or The Toxic Avenger movies, I got swept up in that sweet nostalgic feel when I played Toxic Crusaders. The familiar gameplay loops, Saturday morning cartoon feel, and enemies that blinked away when they were defeated were screaming out to some of the best parts of my childhood. Even without that experience, I felt right at home playing Toxic Crusaders in large part because they didn’t try to reinvent the wheel.

For people with more experience with Toxic Crusaders’ storied history, this revival of an old classic will likely feel right at home.  Just like the old cartoon and video game, The Toxic Crusaders are back in Tromaville, named after Troma Entertainment, the company that first created The Toxic Avenger. They’re fighting their old nemesis, Dr. Killemoff and his forces from Smogula.  Toxic Crusaders is launching this year on PC and current-gen consoles.

Kirk Mephisto

Kirk Mephisto was probably the newest game in terms of development. The trailer for Kirk Mephisto only dropped one week before LVL UP and there is no release window to look forward to just yet.  Still, Kirk Mephisto impressed me with the sheer level of fun it provides.  Kirk Mephisto is a hack-and-slash game similar to the game styles of Spider-Man or Devil May Cry.  Each of the four playable characters is a warrior that is also a different bug, such as a worm, as the name Mephisto implies, or a butterfly, as you can see in the splash screen on Steam.

As you go through the demo, there are different combat encounters. As my son and I played through the game, we spoke with someone working on it, who explained that the early stages teach you how the combat systems work. I knew we had a winner when my son — a 13-year-old boy who at 9 years old was getting the platinum trophy in Spider-Man games — came across a boss fight, died almost instantly, and was disappointed when I told him we should give other people a chance to play.

Kirk Mephisto’s announcement on X says that there will be a story mode, secret missions, and a challenge mode.  Currently there is no release date but it can be added to your Steam wishlist.  

There were easily a dozen games available to try out in The MIX. While I didn’t have the opportunity to play every single game, I did try out quite a few.  There were some that weren’t really holding my attention but most of the games I tried, I had the ability to enjoy.  These four games, however, were true standouts in the crowd and I couldn’t be happier to have shared just a taste of what’s coming to indie gaming soon.

Justin Harmon's round-up:

#Drive Rally

I probably spent more time trying #Drive Rally out versus any of the others on this list. From the start, I felt like I was back in front of an old CRT screen with a Sega Saturn controller in my hand playing Sega Rally GT. From the jump #Drive Rally feels like a spiritual successor to the Sega Rally series and that might be why I enjoyed it as much as I did.

Track design was tough, though it got easier as I tried out different cars over multiple attempts. Controlling my rally car through the different terrain and conditions started out as a great challenge. With a nice selection of available cars with different driving abilities and multiple tracks to test myself on, I just wanted to try again and again, with as many different options as possible. Art and audio design helped to make the world I was in more immersive, that I didn’t notice I was driving through towns and probably causing untold amounts of property damage.

All of it comes together in a package that I cannot wait to get my hands on further and dive into what the full game has to offer. 

D-Topia (Demo Available Now)

D-Topia was an interesting title to try out. The Steam page description, in short, does say that D-Topia is a “gentle-paced puzzle adventure” and just from playing the demo at LvL Up, that description is true to its word. By the end of the demo, I started to see the full picture of what the development team was aiming for here. I’m a sucker for puzzle games and the demo for D-Topia had some fun puzzles to try and play though. 

However, it is the RPG elements along the way that captured me and has me wanting to give the full game a spin when it finally releases. Puzzles might be the main hook to get eyeballs on the merchandise, but the RPG side of the game is what really drove me to want to play more. I was left wanting to learn more about the world created by the development team. Combine that with some great artwork, level design, easy to learn controls and interesting character interactions, everything adds to the charm to create an immersive world that I want to dive more into and keep playing. 

I hope to be able to get more hands-on time with D-Topia in the future to really open more of the full experience of what the creators of this beautiful world have in store for us fans. 

Demi and the Fractured Dream

Visually, this was the most stunning game I got to try at LvL Up Expo. Demi and the Fractured Dream reminds me a lot of some of the Zelda titles in the way I moved and played through the available level in the demo. Something about the overall art style and the environmental immersion hooked me into wanting to learn more about the world I had been thrust into and it is absolutely gorgeous. 

Battle mechanics, movement and exploration are all fun aspects that I thoroughly enjoyed while testing out the demo. I wasn’t sure exactly what I was in for when picking the controller up for the first time, but it did not take me very long to want to just keep playing. During the demo, I traversed through a dungeon, solved some puzzles, found myself going into areas I probably shouldn’t have been, fought some enemies and squared up with the level's boss. Sounds like a pretty good day in my book. 

Overall, Demi and the Fractured Dream is probably my most anticipated game from the small selection I was able to play during LvL Up weekend. And with a planned release date of later this year, I will wait with great eagerness for that date to come so I can play more and more of this game. 


Mike BC

Mike BC is in Las Vegas, NV where he is a husband, father, minister, and gamer (in that order). Currently, he plays a lot of Elite: Dangerous Odyssey, Fortnite, Fall Guys, and enjoys games on the Nintendo Switch. You can follow him at CMDRErekSprax on Twitch!
